html:
X

On entering the village, Yagna at once could see that something out of the ordinary was going on. The dogs in the farmyard were barking in great excitement; the little ones, hiding in the orchards, peeped out from behind the trees and hedges; the people, though it was yet far from sunset, were fast coming in from the fields; women were whispering together in groups; every face bore an expression of disquietude, and in every eye there was a look of alarm and suspense.

тАЬWhat has come about?тАЭ she asked the Balcerek girl, peering round the corner of her hut.

тАЬI cannot say; belike soldiers coming from the forest.тАЭ

тАЬJesu Maria! Soldiers!тАЭ And her knees trembled with terror.

тАЬYoung Klemba,тАЭ added the Prychek girl, as she ran by, тАЬsays they are Cossacks from Vola.тАЭ

In great dismay, Yagna hurried on to her cabin, where her mother, sitting on the threshold, and spinning, was in earnest talk with several women.

тАЬWe have both seen the same thingтБатАФthe men sitting in the porch, and their leader with the priest inside the house.тАЭ

тАЬAnd they have sent the organistтАЩs lad Michael to fetch the Voyt.тАЭ

тАЬThe Voyt! then it can be no trifle. Ho, ho! something is in the wind!тАЭ

тАЬIt may be they have only come to collect the taxes.тАЭ

тАЬWith such a number of men? No, they come surely for something more than that.тАЭ

тАЬPerhaps; but, mark my words, they are here for no good!тАЭ

Yagustynka came up. тАЬI,тАЭ she said, тАЬcan tell you why they have come.тАЭ

All crowded round her, stretching their necks out like so many geese.

тАЬThey have come to take us women into the army!тАЭ she cried with a croaking laugh that no one took up; and Dominikova remarked sourly:

тАЬYe must always be making some wretched joke!тАЭ

тАЬIt is you that are always making mountains of molehills! You quake so, your teeth are well-nigh falling out of your heads; yet all are greedy to hear that something is to hap! Much do I trouble about the gendarmes!тАЭ

Thereupon Ploshkova, pushing forward her portly figure, began telling them how тАЬsomething had come over her as soon as she saw those carts.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬBe quiet! Here comes Gregory and the Voyt, running at full speed towards the priestтАЩs house.тАЭ

Their eyes followed the two moving figures on the farther side of the pond.

тАЬAha! Gregory too is wanted!тАЭ

They were wrong. Gregory only pushed his brother in, but stayed himself to look at the carts drawn up there, and to question the drivers who were sitting in the porch. Then, in great distress, he ran to Matthew, who was working at StahoтАЩs cabin, and sitting astride on one of the roof-beams, while cutting hollows in it to fix the rafters.

тАЬNot gone yet?тАЭ he asked, cutting away as before.

тАЬNo; and the worst is, we cannot tell for whom they have come.тАЭ

тАЬSome evil thing is certainly at hand,тАЭ old Bylitsa stammered.

тАЬPerchance they come about our meeting. The District Official threatened us then, and the gendarmes have been to and fro, seeking to find out who it is that eggs on the Lipka folk,тАЭ Matthew said, slipping down to the ground.

тАЬThen they are likely to have come for me!тАЭ Gregory rejoined, suddenly breathless with apprehension.

тАЬNo, I think they mean to seize Roch!тАЭ Staho asserted.

тАЬTrue, they have inquired about him once already: how could I let that slip my memory?тАЭ He felt relieved for himself; but at once said, in distress for the otherтАЩs fate:

тАЬNo doubt, if they have come for anyone, тАЩtis for him!тАЭ

тАЬWell, but shall we let him be taken?тАЭ shouted Matthew. тАЬHim, that is so truly a father to us all!тАЭ

тАЬAlas! we cannot resist them, it is not to be thought of.тАЭ

тАЬLet him hide somewhereтБатАФand first let us warn him instantly.тАЭ

тАЬBut peradventure,тАЭ Staho remarked diffidently, тАЬthey may have come on some other errandтБатАФthe VoytтАЩs business, for instance.тАЭ

тАЬHe must at all events be warned,тАЭ cried Gregory; and, rushing out into the rye, and working around several gardens, he soon reached BorynaтАЩs hut.

Antek was sitting in the porch, putting jagged edges to some sickles on a small anvil. On hearing what the matter was, he started up in alarm.

тАЬHe has only just come in.тБатАФRoch!тАЭ he cried. тАЬHere, we want you.тАЭ

тАЬWhat is it?тАЭ the old man asked, putting his head out of the window; but before they had time to speak, in dashed Michael, the organistтАЩs lad, panting very hard.

тАЬKnow, Antek, that the gendarmes are coming to you now, and are already at the millpond!тАЭ

тАЬFor me!тАЭ Roch bowed his head with a sigh.

тАЬJesu Maria!тАЭ Hanka shrieked from the threshold, and burst into tears.

тАЬOh, be quiet!тАЭ Antek whispered; he was thinking very hard. тАЬWe must hit upon something.тАЭ

тАЬRoch!тАЭ vociferated Michael, breaking off a large branch and looking daggers. тАЬIтАЩll shout the news through Lipka, and we will not give you up!тАЭ

тАЬNo fooling!тБатАФRoch! Get behind the haystack and into the rye this instant. Wiggle into some furrow, hide yourself well, and stay till I call you.тБатАФQuick! ere they are here!тАЭ

Roch snatched up some papers he had in the room and handed them to Yuzka, who was in bed:

тАЬHide them under yourself, do not give them up,тАЭ he whispered.

And just as he was, without hat or capote, he darted into the orchard and vanished like a stone in the waters: they could just see the rye undulating slightly beyond the haystack.

тАЬNow, Gregory, off with you! Hanka, to your work! Go, MichaelтБатАФand not a word of this!тАЭ Antek commanded, sitting down again to his interrupted labour. Again he set to notching the edges of his reaping-hooks, evenly and calmly as before. Now and again he would hold the edge up to the light, glancing the while in every direction about him; for the barking of the dogs was growing louder, and in a little he could hear the heavy tread of the approaching gendarmes, the jingling of their sabres, and the sound of their voices.

His heart was palpitating, his hands were shaking; yet he managed to go on, notching evenly, regularly, with rhythmical strokes, never raising his eyes till the men were standing before him.

тАЬIs Roch in your hut?тАЭ asked the Voyt, mortally afraid.

Antek looked around at the group, and replied with great deliberation:

тАЬHe must be in the village, I suppose: I have not set eyes on him since this morning.тАЭ

тАЬOpen your doors!тАЭ thundered the commanding officer.

тАЬWhy, they are open!тАЭ Antek growled, getting up from his bench.

The officer and some of his men went in, while the others watched the orchard and outhouses.

About half the village was now outside in the road, looking on in silence, while the cottage was searched and ransacked thoroughly. Antek had to point out and open everything, while Hanka sat by the window with the baby at her breast.

The search was of course fruitless; but they sought everywhere, and were so careful to overlook nothing that one of them even peered under the bed!

Some little books, strapped together, were lying on the table. The officer pounced upon them, and set to examine them with the utmost care.

тАЬHow have ye come by these?тАЭ

тАЬBelike Roch has left them thereтБатАКтБатАж and there they lie.тАЭ

тАЬThe mistress here cannot read,тАЭ the Voyt explained.

тАЬCan anyone amongst you read?тАЭ

тАЬNo,тАЭ Antek returned; тАЬthey teach us at school so well that now no one is able even to spell out the words in our prayer-books!тАЭ

The officer handed the little books to a subordinate, and passed round to the other side of the hut.

тАЬWhatтАЩs here?тБатАФA sick child?тАЭ he said, taking a step towards Yuzka.

тАЬYes. She has been lying there for a couple of weeks: smallpox.тАЭ

He retired hurriedly into the passage.

тАЬWas Roch a lodger in this cabin?тАЭ he asked of the Voyt.

тАЬIn this or any other, according as it struck him: тАЩtis the dziadsтАЩ wont.тАЭ

They peered into every hole and corner, even looking behind the holy images; while Yuzka followed their movements with eyes full of dread, trembling all over. One of them having approached her, she cried out wildly:

тАЬOh, have I hidden him under me? Seek him then here, do!тАЭ

When they had done, Antek went over to their officer, and said very humbly, with a deep bow:

тАЬHas Roch stolen aught, I should like to know?тАЭ

The other, putting his face close to AntekтАЩs, replied with a stare, and laying stress on each word:

тАЬBe it but found that you have concealed him, and ye shall go on a journey together, both of you!тБатАФDo you hear?тАЭ

тАЬI hear indeed, but cannot think what all this means.тАЭ And he scratched his head, as if much perplexed.

The officer shot an angry look at him, and left the cabin.

They went round to many another, looking here and there, asking questions of many a one, until sundown; when, the roads filling with home-driven cattle, they went back empty-handed.

Now the village breathed freely, and people began telling of the searchesтБатАФat the KlembasтАЩтБатАФat GregoryтАЩsтБатАФat MatthewтАЩsтБатАФand how each had seen things better than anybody else, and had not been frightened in the least, but had annoyed and bantered the gendarmes to the utmost!

But Antek, once alone with Hanka, said to her, dropping his voice:

тАЬThis is a wretched business, I see: there will be no keeping him in our cabin any longer.тАЭ

тАЬWhat, turn him out? So holy a man? One that does so much good?тАЭ

тАЬA curse on it all! I am sick of it!тАЭ he cried, unable to find any way out of the quandary. But Gregory came presently, along with Matthew, and they held a consultation, locked up together in the barn: the cabin, continually full of callers for news, was no fit place.

When they came out, it was quite dark. Hanka had milked the cows, and Pete was back from the forest. Antek got the britzka and directly, while Gregory and Matthew went out, ostensibly to look everywhere for Roch, in reality to mislead the people of the village.

They were indeed all surprised at the quest, having made sure that Roch lay somewhere concealed on BorynaтАЩs premises. But the two friends gave out that he had left BorynaтАЩs directly after dinner and had not been heard of since.

тАЬLucky for him, or he would be journeying in chains ere now!тАЭ

So it became generally known (as they had planned) that Roch had not been seen in Lipka since noon.

People were glad, and said amongst themselves: тАЬHe guessed what was awaiting him, and is off тАШto the land where pepper grows.тАЩтАКтАЭ

тАЬLet him not come back, I say; we do not want him,тАЭ old Ploshka growled.

Matthew snarled back at him: тАЬIs he in your way? Has he wronged you in aught?тАЭ

тАЬHe disturbed the peace and troubled Lipka not a little. We all may yet suffer on his account.тАЭ

тАЬThen why not seize on him, you, and give him up?тАЭ

тАЬLong ago we should have done so, had we any understanding!тАЭ

Matthew uttered a curse, and would have flown at him; they held him back, but with difficulty. And then, it being late, they went each man to his own cabin.

Antek was awaiting this moment, when the roads were deserted, and everybody was supping at home, and the scent of fried bacon was wafted abroad with the sound of merry talk and the tinkling of spoons in the dishes; then he brought Roch to the room where Yuzka lay; but he would not have a candle lit.

The old man snatched a hasty meal, put on what clothes he had left in the hut, and said farewell to the women. Hanka fell at his feet, and Yuzka wept and wailed piteously.

тАЬGod be with you! we may meet once more!тАЭ he said in a tearful voice, pressing them paternally to his breast, and kissing them on the forehead; but, Antek urging him to make haste, he once more blessed the women and children, crossed himself, and went out to the stile by the haystack.

тАЬThe britzka is waiting at SimonтАЩs hut in Podlesie, and Matthew will drive for you.тАЭ

тАЬBut I must still pay a visit here in Lipka.тБатАФWhere are we to meet?тАЭ

тАЬAt the crucifix by the forest, whither we are going at once.тАЭ

тАЬThatтАЩs well, for I have yet many things to speak of with Gregory.тАЭ

And presently he was unseen and inaudible.

Antek put the horses to, placed a bushel of rye and a whole sack of potatoes in the britzka, conferred for some time apart with Vitek, and then said, for all to hear:

тАЬVitek! drive over to SzymekтАЩs hut with the cart, and then come back: do ye hear?тАЭ

The ladтАЩs eyes blazed, and he started off at such a pace that Antek called after him:

тАЬSlower, you rogue, or youтАЩll lame the horses!тАЭ

Roch had meantime crept stealthily to DominikovaтАЩs, where he had left a few things, and shut himself up in the inner room.

Andrew was on the watch by the roadside, and Yagna every now and then looked out into the enclosure, while the old woman, sitting in the front room, listened, trembling all over.

It took him some time before he came out to talk a little with Dominikova by herself; then he wanted to take up his bag and start off. But Yagna insisted on carrying it for him, at least to the forest. He agreed, and, taking leave of the others, went out into the fields, and slowly along the narrow pathways, with noiseless caution.

The night was clear and starlit; the lands lay hushed in slumber, with only now and then a sound of fitful barking.

They were nearing the forest, when Roch, coming to a standstill, took YagnaтАЩs hand.

тАЬHear me, Yagna,тАЭ he said in a kindly tone, тАЬand take to heart what I am going to say.тАЭ

She lent an ear, though agitated by an unpleasant sense of foreboding.

Then, just as a priest might speak in confession, he talked to her of her doingsтБатАКтБатАж with AntekтБатАКтБатАж with the VoytтБатАКтБатАж and most of all with Yanek.

She listened in deep humiliation, with averted face covered with blushes; but when he named Yanek, she raised her head defiantly.

тАЬWith him I have done no evil whatsoever!тАЭ

He pointed out gently to her the temptations to which they were exposing themselvesтБатАКтБатАж the sins and scandals to which the Evil One might give occasion thereby.

But she hearkened to him no longer; her mind was full of Yanek only: unconsciously her bright red lips were murmuring with ardent and frenzied love:

тАЬYanek, O Yanek!тАЭ

Her glowing eyes gazed afar, and circled in fancy over his adored head.

тАЬOh, I would go with him to the ends of the earth!тАЭ she declared, not knowing what she said. At the words, Roch shuddered, cast one look at those wide-open eyes, and held his peace thenceforward.

At the edge of the wood, just by the crucifix, several capotes were seen to glimmer white. Roch stopped, full of misgiving:

тАЬWho is there?тАЭ

тАЬOnly weтБатАФyour friends!тАЭ

тАЬI am tired, and must rest awhile,тАЭ he said, sitting down amongst them. Yagna gave up the bag to him, and seated herself not far off, at the foot of the crucifix, in the deep shadow of the branches.

тАЬWell, may your troubles at least come to an end here!тАЭ

тАЬThe worst of all will come,тАЭ said Antek, тАЬnow that you go from us.тАЭ

тАЬBut it may be, it well may be, that I shall return one day!тАЭ

Here Matthew exploded. тАЬBlood of a dog!тАЭ he cried; тАЬto hunt men down soтБатАКтБатАж as if they were mangy curs!тАЭ

Gregory moaned. тАЬAnd why, Lord God, why?тАЭ

тАЬBecause,тАЭ Roch declared with solemn emphasis, тАЬI want truth and righteousness for the people!тАЭ

тАЬHard is every manтАЩs lot; but that of the righteous is harder!тАЭ

тАЬDo not mourn, Gregory; evil will be changed into good.тАЭ

тАЬSo I think; тАЩtis hard to fancy that all we do is in vain.тАЭ

тАЬWhile weтАЩre awaiting the summer, the wolves will eat up our horses,тАЭ Antek sighed, peering into the darkness at the white blot which was YagnaтАЩs face.

тАЬBut I say unto you: тАШWhoso plucks up the weeds and sows good seeds, great riches shall win, when harvest comes in!тАЩтАКтАЭ

тАЬAnd if he fail?тБатАФSuch things have been.тАЭ

тАЬYea, but he that sows, sows in the hope of gathering in a hundredfold.тАЭ

тАЬSurely, for who would care to lose his toil?тАЭ

And they pondered these things deep in their hearts.

The wind was up now, the birch-trees murmured above them; a rustling sound came out of the forest, while the voice of the waving corn rose up to them from the fields. The moon floated along a pathway in the sky, made up of a double row of white clouds; the trees flung shadows mingled with patches of brightness; goatsuckers passed over their heads with a noiseless circling flight. Their hearts were very full of sadness.

Yagna shed tears in silence: she could not have said why.

тАЬWherefore do you sorrow?тАЭ he asked, laying his hand paternally on her head.

But the others too, all gloomy and cheerless, sat with their eyes fixed upon Roch, whom they now held for a man of God. He was sitting beneath the cross, from which the Crucified seemed to bend forward to bless his white weary head.

Then he spoke these words to them, full of hope and confidence:

тАЬFear naught for me. I am only a unitтБатАФone blade of corn in a fruitful field. If they take me, and I perish, what of that?тБатАФSo many more remain!тБатАФeach of us ready to die for the Cause!тБатАКтБатАж And the time cometh when there will be thousands of them, from town and country, from cottage and from manor, all incessantly giving up their lives, one after another, piled and heaped together, the stones that are to form into the Holy Church of our desire! And that Church, I say unto you, shall stand and last forever; and no power of evil shall prevail against it, because it will be built up completely with blood and loving sacrifice!тАЭ

Then he told them how no drop of blood, nay, not one single tear, would fall in vain, nor any endeavour be without its fruit; and how on every side, as from a soil abundantly manured, new forces, new defenders, and new victims would spring forth, until that blessed day should dawnтБатАФthat sacred day, the day of resurrection and of justice and of truth for all the nation!

He spoke with glowing enthusiasm; often, too, of such high matters that they could not understand all that he said; but his fire inflamed them also, and their hearts leaped up and were exalted by his words in mighty faith and longing. Antek said at last:

тАЬO God!тБатАФBe ye our leader: I will follow you even to death!тАЭ

тАЬWe all will follow you and trample down whatever may resist us!тАЭ

тАЬWho can withstand us and prevail? Let him but try!тАЭ

So they all spoke, till he was forced to hush their violent words and, drawing them still closer, and whispering, say what that longed-for day would be, and how its coming would be hastened by their labours.

He told them many a thing they had not dreamed of, and they listened breathless, full of dread and joy at once; and every word of his gave them the thrill of faith which one feels at the Communion Table. He opened heaven before them, and made Paradise appear visibly to their eyes; their souls fell prostrate in deep ecstasy, their eyes beheld ineffable wonders, and in their hearts the sweet, sweet hymn of Hope was heard.

тАЬAnd it is in your power to realize all this,тАЭ he ended, when quite tired out. The moon was just eclipsed behind a cloud; the sky was grey, the landscape murky; the woods gave forth their inarticulate utterances, and the cornfields rustled and shook as if with fear. Afar there was a noise of dogs that barked. And still they sat there, silent and subdued, listening in rapt attention, inebriated with the words he had said, and feeling as one who has just taken some great vow may feel.

тАЬIt is time: I must go!тАЭ he said, and, rising, embraced each of them, pressing them to his heart. They could hardly keep back their tears when he knelt down, said a short prayer, and prostrated himself with both arms on the breast of that holy motherтБатАФthe land which he might perhaps never see again. Yagna sobbed aloud, and the others were struggling with deep emotions.

Such was their parting.

Antek alone went straight back to Lipka, along with Yagna; the others disappeared in the shadows at the edge of the forest.

They long walked on in silence. Then he said: тАЬBeware and say naught to anyone of that which you have heard.тАЭ

тАЬAm I, then, a pedlar of news from hut to hut?тАЭ She was offended.

тАЬAnd,тАЭ he added, with stern significance, тАЬGod forbid that the Voyt should hear anything of this!тАЭ

She answered only by hurrying on; but he would not let her go, and strode on by her side, again and again glancing at her indignant face, bedewed with tears.

The moon shone out again, silvering the narrow pathway where they walked abreast, and throwing across it the black distorted shadows of the trees. Suddenly his heart throbbed fast; his arms quivered with a sense of greedy desire, and he took a step nearer to her side.тБатАФHe might have gathered her to his breast with a sweeping grasp. But he did notтБатАФhe durst not. Her stubborn and disdainful silence held him back, and he only said to her bitterly:

тАЬYou seem as if you wanted to get away from me.тАЭ

тАЬBecause I do! Someone might see us together, and tongues would wag.тАЭ

тАЬAre you in a hurry to fly to anyone else?тАЭ

тАЬI am. What is to prevent me? Am I not a widow?тАЭ

тАЬThey say (no idle talk, I see) that you prepare to keep house for a certain priest.тАЭ

Swift as the wind, she rushed away, her tears falling in torrents down her cheeks.
